paragraphs_en: Taiyi (the Grand One — the cosmic pivot whose movements through nine mystical palaces were tracked for divination, akin to a wandering pole star) always begins, on the day of the winter solstice, by dwelling in the Palace of Yezhe (Leaf-Fall and Hibernation) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Tianliu (Celestial Retention) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Cangmen (Granary Gate) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Yinluo for forty-five days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Tiangong (Celestial Palace) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Xuanwei (Mysterious Repose) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Cangguo (Granary Fruit) for forty-six days. The next day it dwells in the Palace of Xinluo (New Luo) for forty-five days. The next day it returns once more to dwell in the Palace of Yezhe — and this marks the return of the winter solstice.

Taiyi wanders daily. On the day of the winter solstice it takes up residence in the Palace of Yezhe; one counts the days spent in each location, proceeding from the first palace through to the ninth, and then returning again to the first. It is always thus, without cease, ending only to begin anew.

On the day Taiyi shifts its residence, Heaven invariably responds with wind and rain. If the wind and rain arrive precisely on that day, it is auspicious — the year will be bountiful, the people at peace, and illness scarce. If the wind and rain come before that day, there will be excessive rain; if they come after, there will be drought. Should Taiyi undergo change on the day of the winter solstice, the omen concerns the ruler; should it undergo change on the day of the spring equinox, the omen concerns the ministers; should it undergo change on the day it resides in the central palace, the omen concerns the officials; should it undergo change on the day of the autumn equinox, the omen concerns the generals; should it undergo change on the day of the summer solstice, the omen concerns the common people. What is meant by 'undergoing change' is this: on the days when Taiyi dwells in these five palaces, if disease-bearing winds snap trees and scatter sand and stones, then, according to what each palace governs, one may divine matters of noble and base rank. One divines according to the direction from which the wind arises: wind arising from the direction in which Taiyi currently dwells is called a replete wind; it governs life, and nourishes and rears the myriad things. Wind arising from the direction opposite Taiyi's dwelling is called a vacuous wind; it injures people, governing death and harm. One must carefully watch for vacuous winds and avoid them. Hence the sage's daily practice of avoiding vacuous evil follows this same principle — avoiding it as one would avoid arrows and stones, so that evil cannot cause harm. This is what is meant.

Accordingly, when Taiyi enters and takes its stand in the central palace, it holds court over the eight winds, and by this one divines fortune and misfortune. When the wind comes from the south, it is called the Great Weak Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the heart and appears without in the vessels, and its qi governs heat. When the wind comes from the southwest, it is called the Scheming Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the spleen and appears without in the flesh, and its qi governs weakness. When the wind comes from the west, it is called the Rigid Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the lung and appears without in the skin, and its qi governs dryness. When the wind comes from the northwest, it is called the Breaking Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the small intestine and appears without in the Hand Taiyang vessel; when the vessel is severed there is overflow, and when the vessel is blocked there is binding and non-communication, so that sudden death readily follows. When the wind comes from the north, it is called the Great Rigid Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the kidney and appears without in the bones and in the sinews of the shoulders and back, and its qi governs cold. When the wind comes from the northeast, it is called the Baleful Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the large intestine and appears without at the two flanks, beneath the armpit bones, and at the joints of the limbs. When the wind comes from the east, it is called the Ying-wu Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the liver and appears without at the sinew-knots, and its qi governs bodily dampness. When the wind comes from the southeast, it is called the Weak Wind; when it injures a person, it lodges within in the stomach and appears without in the flesh, and its qi governs heaviness of the body. These eight winds all come from the direction of vacuity, and it is only then that they can cause illness in a person. When three vacuities strike together, this brings about sudden, fulminant disease and abrupt death. When two are replete and one vacuous, illness manifests as painful stranguria and exposure to cold and heat. If one is exposed to a place doubly damp, this brings about wilting weakness. Hence the sage avoids wind as one avoids arrows and stones. When a person has three vacuities and is struck squarely by evil wind, this results in collapse and hemiplegia.

Huangdi said: 'I have heard from you, Master, of the nine needles — a subject vast and profound indeed — yet I still cannot fully grasp it. May I ask: from what did the nine needles arise, and for what reason were they so named?' Qibo said: 'The nine needles embody the great numbers of Heaven and Earth; they begin at one and end at nine. Thus it is said: the first takes Heaven as its model; the second takes Earth as its model; the third takes Man as its model; the fourth takes the seasons as its model; the fifth takes the musical notes as its model; the sixth takes the pitch-pipes as its model; the seventh takes the stars as its model; the eighth takes the winds as its model; and the ninth takes the fields of the earth as its model.'

Huangdi said: 'How do the needles correspond to the number nine?' Qibo said: 'When the sages established the numbers of Heaven and Earth, they took one and multiplied it ninefold, and thereby established the nine fields. Nine multiplied ninefold gives nine nines, eighty-one, from which the pitch of the Yellow Bell was derived — and it is to this number that the needles correspond.'

The first corresponds to Heaven. Heaven is yang. Among the five viscera, the one that corresponds to Heaven is the lung, for the lung is the canopy over the five viscera and six bowels; the skin, being the lung's outward correlate, is the yang of the body. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its head large and its tip sharp, so that it cannot penetrate deeply, and so that the yang qi may be released.

The second corresponds to Earth. That in the human body which corresponds to soil is the flesh. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft tubular and its tip round, so that it cannot injure the partitions of the flesh — for if injured, the qi would become exhausted.

The third corresponds to Man. That by which a person comes to life is the blood and vessels. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft thick and its tip round, so that it can press upon the vessel without sinking into it, in order to summon forth the qi and allow the evil qi alone to be expelled.

The fourth corresponds to the seasons. The seasons are when the qi of the four seasons and eight winds lodges within the channels and network vessels, giving rise to tumor-like afflictions. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft tubular and its tip bladed, so that it can drain heat and let blood, thereby exhausting stubborn and chronic disease.

The fifth corresponds to the musical notes. The notes mark the division between winter and summer, divided at the hours of zi and wu, where yin and yang separate and cold and heat contend; when these two qi clash, they combine to form welling-abscess and pus. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its tip like the blade of a sword, so that it can drain great accumulations of pus.

The sixth corresponds to the pitch-pipes. The pitch-pipes regulate yin and yang and the four seasons, and correspond to the twelve channels; this is where vacuous evil lodges in the channels and network vessels, producing sudden impediment disorders. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip must be as fine as a hair yet rounded at its point, with the shaft slightly thickened at the middle, so as to draw out sudden and fulminant qi.

The seventh corresponds to the stars. The stars correspond to the seven orifices of the person, where evil lodges in the channels and becomes painful impediment, joined with the channels and network vessels. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip should be like the proboscis of a mosquito or gnat; it should be inserted quietly and slowly, retained gently for a long while, so that the upright qi may follow it and the true and the evil qi both depart together; the needle is then withdrawn, allowing the patient to be nourished and restored.

The eighth corresponds to the winds. The winds correspond to the eight joints of a person's thighs and arms. The vacuous winds of the eight solar terms, the eight winds, injure a person by lodging within, between the bone joints, the lower back and spine, and the interstitial spaces, producing deep impediment. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, one must make its shaft long and its tip bladed, so that it can reach deep evil and distant impediment.

The ninth corresponds to the fields. The fields correspond to the spaces between a person's joints and skin. This is where excessive evil overflows through the body, resembling the swelling of wind-water, and lingers, unable to pass through the pivoting mechanisms of the great joints. Hence, in fashioning the needle for this purpose, the tip should be like a rod, its point slightly rounded, so as to draw out great qi that cannot pass through the joints.

Huangdi said: 'Do the needles have fixed measures of length?' Qibo said: 'The first is called the chan needle — a broad-headed needle modeled after the sewing needle; it tapers abruptly to a point one and a half cun from its tip, and measures one cun and six fen in length; it governs heat located in the head and body. The second is called the round needle; it is modeled after the felting needle, its shaft tubular and its tip egg-shaped, one cun and six fen in length; it governs disorders of qi between the flesh partitions. The third is called the blunt needle; it is modeled after the sharp point of a millet grain, three and a half cun in length; it governs pressing upon the vessel to summon the qi and expel evil. The fourth is called the lancet needle; it is modeled after the felting needle, its shaft tubular and its tip bladed, one cun and six fen in length; it governs draining heat from welling-abscess and letting blood. The fifth is called the sword-tip needle; it is modeled after the edge of a sword, two and a half fen in width and four cun in length; it governs great welling-abscess and pus, where two heats contend with one another. The sixth is called the round-sharp needle; it is modeled after a fine, hair-thin needle, its tip made slightly thicker while its shaft is made slightly thinner, so that it may penetrate deeply; it is one cun and six fen in length, and governs the treatment of welling-abscess and impediment. The seventh is called the filiform needle; it takes its form from a fine hair, is one cun and six fen in length, and governs cold and heat, pain and impediment located in the network vessels. The eighth is called the long needle; it is modeled after a long, slender probe, seven cun in length, and governs the extraction of deep evil and distant impediment. The ninth is called the great needle; it is modeled after the lancet needle, its tip made slightly rounded, four cun in length, and governs the extraction of great qi that cannot pass through the joints. Thus the forms of the needles are complete — these are the rules governing the size and length of the nine needles.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear how the body's form corresponds to the nine fields.' Qibo said: 'Allow me to explain how the body's form corresponds to the nine fields. The left foot corresponds to the Establishment of Spring, its days being wuyin and jichou. The left rib-side corresponds to the Spring Equinox, its day being yimao. The left hand corresponds to the Establishment of Summer, its days being wuchen and jisi. The chest, throat, and head correspond to the Summer Solstice, its day being bingwu. The right hand corresponds to the Establishment of Autumn, its days being wushen and jiwei. The right rib-side corresponds to the Autumn Equinox, its day being xinyou. The right foot corresponds to the Establishment of Winter, its days being wuxu and jihai. The lower back, sacrum, and lower orifices correspond to the Winter Solstice, its day being renzi. The six bowels and, below the diaphragm, the three viscera correspond to the central region, and its great prohibition falls on the days on which Taiyi resides there, as well as on all wu and ji days. In all nine of these cases, one does well to watch for the times when the eight solar terms fall. If welling-abscess or swelling appears on the left, right, upper, or lower part of the body governed by these regions, and one wishes to treat it, one must not lance and treat it on the very day corresponding to that region — these are what are called the heaven-forbidden days.'

When the body is at ease but the mind distressed, disease arises in the vessels, and it is treated with moxibustion and needling. When the body is distressed but the mind at ease, disease arises in the sinews, and it is treated with hot compress and stretching exercises. When both body and mind are at ease, disease arises in the flesh, and it is treated with needle and stone. When both body and mind are distressed, disease arises in the throat, and it is treated with sweet-flavored medicinals. When a person is repeatedly startled and frightened, so that the sinews and vessels fail to communicate, disease arises as numbness and insensitivity, and it is treated with massage and medicinal wine. This is what is meant by the body's condition.
As for the qi of the five viscera: the heart governs belching; the lung governs coughing; the liver governs excessive talking; the spleen governs swallowing; and the kidney governs yawning.

As for the qi of the six bowels: the gallbladder governs anger; the stomach governs rebellious qi and foul emissions; the large and small intestines govern diarrhea; when the bladder fails to control itself, this governs enuresis; and when the lower burner overflows, this governs water swelling.

The five flavors: sour enters the liver, pungent enters the lung, bitter enters the heart, sweet enters the spleen, salty enters the kidney, and bland enters the stomach — these are called the five flavors.

The five concentrations: when essential qi concentrates in the liver, this causes sorrow; when it concentrates in the heart, this causes joy; when it concentrates in the lung, this causes grief; when it concentrates in the kidney, this causes fear; when it concentrates in the spleen, this causes apprehension — these are called the concentrations of the five essential qi within the viscera.

The five aversions: the liver detests wind; the heart detests heat; the lung detests cold; the kidney detests dryness; and the spleen detests dampness — these are what the qi of the five viscera detest.

The five fluids: the heart governs sweat; the liver governs tears; the lung governs nasal mucus; the kidney governs spittle; and the spleen governs saliva — these are the sources from which the five fluids emerge.

The five forms of taxation: prolonged looking injures the blood; prolonged lying injures the qi; prolonged sitting injures the flesh; prolonged standing injures the bones; and prolonged walking injures the sinews — these are the illnesses caused by the five prolonged exertions.

The five destinations: sour flavor travels to the sinews; pungent flavor travels to the qi; bitter flavor travels to the blood; salty flavor travels to the bones; and sweet flavor travels to the flesh — these are called the five destinations.

The five restrictions: when disease is in the sinews, one should not eat sour flavors; when disease is in the qi, one should not eat pungent flavors; when disease is in the bones, one should not eat salty flavors; when disease is in the blood, one should not eat bitter flavors; when disease is in the flesh, one should not eat sweet flavors. Even if the mouth craves and desires such flavors, one must not indulge in excess — one must restrain oneself. This is called the five restrictions.
The five arisings: yin disease arises in the bones; yang disease arises in the blood; yin disease arises in the flesh; yang disease arises in winter; yin disease arises in summer.

The five evils: when evil enters yang, it causes mania; when evil enters yin, it causes blood impediment; when evil enters yang and shifts, it causes withdrawal epilepsy; when evil enters yin and shifts, it causes muteness; when yang evil enters into yin, the illness is marked by quietude; when yin evil emerges into yang, the illness is marked by irritability and anger.
The five viscera: the heart stores the spirit; the lung stores the corporeal soul (po); the liver stores the ethereal soul (hun); the spleen stores intent; and the kidney stores essence and will.
The five governances: the heart governs the vessels; the lung governs the skin; the liver governs the sinews; the spleen governs the muscles; and the kidney governs the bones.

Yangming is abundant in both blood and qi; Taiyang is abundant in blood but scant in qi; Shaoyang is abundant in qi but scant in blood; Taiyin is abundant in blood but scant in qi; Jueyin is abundant in blood but scant in qi; and Shaoyin is abundant in qi but scant in blood. Hence it is said: when needling Yangming, let out both blood and qi; when needling Taiyang, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; when needling Shaoyang, let out qi but avoid depleting the blood; when needling Taiyin, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; when needling Jueyin, let out blood but avoid depleting the qi; and when needling Shaoyin, let out qi but avoid depleting the blood.

The Foot Yangming and Taiyin channels stand in interior-exterior relation; the Foot Shaoyang and Jueyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Foot Taiyang and Shaoyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation — these are the yin and yang pairings of the foot channels. The Hand Yangming and Taiyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Hand Shaoyang and Pericardium (Heart Ruler) channels stand in exterior-interior relation; the Hand Taiyang and Shaoyin channels stand in exterior-interior relation — these are the yin and yang pairings of the hand channels.

title_en: Discourse on the Dew of the Year
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The canonical texts say that being injured by summer-heat in summer causes malarial disease in autumn, and that the onset of malarial disease follows a fixed schedule — what is the reason for this?' Qibo replied: 'The evil lodges in Fengfu (the Wind Mansion), and the disease then travels downward along the spine. The defensive qi, over the course of a day and night, always converges fully at Fengfu; on the following day it descends one vertebral segment, and so the onset of the attack each day comes progressively later — this is because the evil first lodged in the spine and back. Thus, each time the defensive qi reaches Fengfu, the interstices open; when the interstices open, the evil qi enters; when the evil qi enters, the disease erupts — this is why the onset comes progressively later each day. As the defensive qi travels down from Fengfu, descending one segment per day, by the twenty-first day it reaches down to the tailbone, and on the twenty-second day it enters the interior of the spine and pours into the Fuchong (Hidden Penetrating) Vessel; traveling for nine days, it emerges within Quepen, the supraclavicular fossa, and its qi then moves upward — hence the disease gradually intensifies as time goes on. When the evil strikes internally against the five viscera and connects laterally with the membrane-source, its pathway is long and its qi lies deep, so its movement is slow and it cannot erupt daily; hence it accumulates over the following day before erupting.'

Huangdi said: 'When the defensive qi reaches Fengfu each time, the interstices open, and once opened, the evil enters. Yet if the defensive qi descends one segment each day, then it will no longer coincide with Fengfu — how is this?' Qibo said: 'Fengfu has no fixed location. Wherever the defensive qi responds, it must open the interstices there; the joint-segment where the qi lodges becomes, at that moment, its mansion.'

Huangdi said: 'Excellent. Now, wind disorders and malarial disease belong to the same category, yet wind persists constantly while malarial disease alone rests according to a fixed schedule — why is this?' Qibo said: 'Wind qi lingers in its location, whereas malarial qi follows the channels and network vessels, sinking down to strike internally; hence it erupts only when the defensive qi responds to it there.' Huangdi said: 'Excellent.'

Huangdi asked Shaoshi: 'I have heard that when the qi of the four seasons and eight winds strikes a person, there is accordingly cold and heat: in cold, the skin becomes tight and the interstices close; in heat, the skin becomes relaxed and the interstices open. Is it through this that thieving wind and evil qi manage to enter? Or must they wait specifically for the vacuous evil of the eight solar terms before they are able to injure a person?' Shaoshi answered: 'It is not so. When thieving wind and evil qi strike a person, they do not do so according to any fixed season; but they must always take advantage of an opening. When they take advantage of an opening, they penetrate deeply, and the illness within reaches its extreme; such illness strikes the person suddenly and violently. When they take advantage of a closing, they penetrate only shallowly and merely linger; such illness develops slowly and gradually.'

Huangdi said: 'There are cases in which cold and warmth are perfectly balanced, and the interstices are not open, and yet sudden illness still occurs — what is the reason for this?' Shaoshi answered: 'Does Your Majesty not know how evil enters? Even in ordinary daily life, the opening and closing, slackening and tightening of the interstices, follow their own regular timing.' Huangdi said: 'May I hear of this?' Shaoshi said: 'Man corresponds to Heaven and Earth, and responds to the sun and moon. Thus, when the moon is full, the ocean waters swell in the west, a person's blood and qi accumulate, the flesh and muscles become full, the skin becomes dense, the body hair becomes firm, the interstices become closely knit, and soot and grime cling to the skin; at such a time, even if one encounters thieving wind, it penetrates only shallowly, not deeply. But when the moon's disk becomes empty, the ocean waters swell in the east, a person's qi and blood become deficient, the defensive qi departs, the body's form stands alone, the flesh and muscles diminish, the skin becomes slack, the interstices open, the body hair withers, the connective tissue thins, and soot and grime fall away; at such a time, if one encounters thieving wind, it penetrates deeply, and the illness it causes strikes the person suddenly and violently.'

Huangdi said: 'There are cases of sudden death and fulminant illness striking without warning — why is this?' Shaoshi answered: 'Those with the three vacuities die suddenly of fulminant disease; those who possess the three repletions cannot be harmed by evil.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the three vacuities.' Shaoshi said: 'Riding upon the decline of the year, encountering the emptiness of the moon, and losing the harmony of the season — and thereby being injured by thieving wind — these together are called the three vacuities. Hence, if in discussing disease one fails to recognize the three vacuities, even a practitioner is thereby rendered crude and unskilled.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the three repletions.' Shaoshi said: 'Encountering the abundance of the year, meeting the fullness of the moon, and obtaining the harmony of the season — then, even in the presence of thieving wind and evil qi, one cannot be endangered by it.' Huangdi said: 'How excellent this discourse! How clear this Way! Let it be stored in the golden casket, and let it be called the three repletions. And yet, this is but the account of a single person.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear why, in certain years, the whole population suffers the same illness together — for what reason is this so?' Shaoshi said: 'This is what is observed through the eight solar terms.' Huangdi said: 'How does one observe this?' Shaoshi said: 'To observe this, one always looks to the day of the winter solstice, when Taiyi takes its stand in the Palace of Yezhe. At its arrival, Heaven invariably responds with wind and rain. If the wind and rain come from the south, this is a vacuous wind, one that steals in and injures people. If it arrives at midnight, the myriad people are all asleep and are not exposed to it, and so in that year the people fall ill but little. If it arrives during the day, the myriad people are all relaxed and off guard, and all are struck by the vacuous wind, and so the people fall ill in great numbers. The vacuous evil enters and lodges in the bones without manifesting outwardly; when the Establishment of Spring arrives, the yang qi bursts forth greatly and the interstices open, and if, on the very day of the Establishment of Spring, wind comes from the west, the myriad people are again all struck by vacuous wind. When these two evils strike one another, the channel qi becomes bound and knotted, and the pulse becomes intermittent. Hence, whenever people encounter such wind and meet with such rain, this is called encountering the dew of the year. When the year is harmonious and thieving winds are few, the people fall ill but little and die but rarely. When the year has abundant thieving wind and evil qi, and cold and warmth are not in harmony, then the people fall ill in great numbers and many die.'

Huangdi said: 'As for the wind of vacuous evil, how does the harm it causes differ between the noble and the humble, and how does one observe it?' Shaoshi answered: 'On the first day of the first month, Taiyi resides in the Palace of Tianliu. If on that day the wind blows from the northwest without rain, many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dawn, the wind blows from the north, then in spring many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dawn, the north wind blows, the number of people who fall ill will be three in ten. If on the first day of the first month, at midday, the wind blows from the north, then in summer many people will die. If on the first day of the first month, at dusk, the wind blows from the north, then in autumn many people will die. If the north wind blows the whole day through, six in ten will fall gravely ill and die. If on the first day of the first month the wind comes from the south, this is called the Land of Drought. If it comes from the west, this is called White Bones, foretelling calamity for the state, with many people dying. If on the first day of the first month the wind comes from the east, it will tear roofs from houses and scatter sand and stones, and the state will suffer great disaster. If on the first day of the first month the wind blows from the southeast, there will be deaths in spring. If on the first day of the first month the weather is mild and warm without wind, grain prices will be low and the people will not fall ill; if the weather is cold and windy, grain prices will be high and the people will fall ill in great numbers. This is what is meant by observing the winds of the year that injure and harm people. If in the second month, on the chou day, there is no wind, the people will suffer greatly from disorders of the heart and abdomen. If in the third month, on the xu day, the weather is not warm, the people will suffer greatly from cold and heat disorders. If in the fourth month, on the si day, the weather is not hot, the people will suffer greatly from jaundice-heat disorders. If in the tenth month, on the shen day, the weather is not cold, the people will suffer greatly from sudden death. In all cases, what is called wind is that which tears roofs from houses, snaps trees, scatters sand and stones, raises the fine hairs of the body, and forces open the interstices.'
